definition: An autonomous AI system that perceives inputs, decides on actions, and executes them — typically using an LLM as its reasoning engine plus tools, memory, and planning.
The core agent cycle: Perceive (gather inputs), Decide (reason about what to do), Act (execute a tool or response). The agent repeats this loop until the task is complete.
A prompting strategy where the agent alternates between Reasoning (thinking through what to do) and Acting (calling a tool), making its thought process visible and traceable.
A feature in modern LLMs that lets the model output a structured request to call a specific function with specific arguments, rather than just generating plain text.
A format used to describe the structure and types of data — in the context of tools, it tells the LLM exactly what arguments a function accepts so the model can call it correctly.
The conversation history and context kept in the LLM's context window for the duration of a single session. It is lost when the session ends.
Persistent storage outside the model (such as a database or vector store) that an agent can read from and write to across multiple sessions.
The information the agent is actively holding and reasoning over right now — essentially the contents of the current context window at any given moment.

Retrieval-Augmented Generation: a technique where relevant documents are fetched from a knowledge base and injected into the prompt, giving the LLM accurate, up-to-date information without retraining.
An agent that can pursue a goal over multiple steps without human guidance, deciding on its own what tools to use and what to do next.
The ability of an agent to call external functions or APIs — such as a web search, calculator, or database query — to get information or take actions beyond generating text.
Large Language Model: a neural network trained on vast amounts of text that can understand and generate human language, serving as the reasoning brain inside most AI agents.
An early autonomous agent prototype that creates, prioritizes, and executes tasks in a loop, demonstrating how an LLM can manage its own task queue to pursue a long-term goal.
One of the first widely known autonomous agent projects that gave GPT-4 memory and tool access, letting it pursue multi-step goals with minimal human input.
Instructions given to the LLM at the start of a conversation that define its persona, capabilities, constraints, and available tools — the agent's standing orders.
A popular Python and JavaScript framework that provides building blocks (chains, agents, memory, tools) for constructing LLM-powered applications and agents.
In LangChain, a Chain is a sequence of steps — such as formatting a prompt, calling the LLM, then parsing the output — composed together into a reusable pipeline.
The practice of carefully crafting the text you give to an LLM — including instructions, examples, and formatting — to get more accurate and useful outputs.
The LangChain component that runs the agent loop: it sends the prompt to the LLM, parses any tool calls, executes those tools, and feeds the results back until the agent is done.
A LangChain decorator that turns a regular Python function into an agent-usable tool, automatically extracting its name, description, and argument schema from the function definition.
A search API designed for LLM agents that returns clean, summarized web results — commonly used as the default web search tool in LangChain agent examples.
A LangChain memory class that stores the full conversation history as a running buffer and injects it into each new prompt, giving the agent access to everything said so far.
A LangChain setting (verbose=True) that prints the agent's internal reasoning steps and tool calls to the console, making it easy to see exactly what the agent is doing.
The basic unit of text that LLMs process — roughly 3-4 characters or 0.75 words. Both inputs and outputs cost tokens, and models have a maximum number they can handle at once.
The maximum number of tokens an LLM can see at one time, including the system prompt, conversation history, and the current message. Information outside this window is invisible to the model.
When an LLM generates text that sounds confident and plausible but is factually wrong or made up. A key challenge in building reliable agents.
An LLM setting (0.0 to 2.0) that controls randomness in outputs. Lower values produce more deterministic, predictable responses; higher values produce more creative, varied ones.
A numerical vector (list of numbers) that represents the meaning of a piece of text. Similar meanings produce similar vectors, enabling semantic search in RAG systems.
An architecture where multiple AI agents work together, each handling a specialized role, to solve problems that are too complex or broad for a single agent.
A multi-agent design where one orchestrator agent breaks down a task and delegates subtasks to specialized worker agents, then combines their results.
A multi-agent design where agents operate as equals, collaborating or competing directly without a central authority managing the process.
A multi-agent design where output from one agent becomes the input to the next, forming an assembly line where each agent performs a specific transformation step.
A multi-agent design where two or more agents argue opposing positions or critique each other's outputs, using disagreement to surface better answers and reduce individual bias.
A design pattern where the agent pauses at critical decision points and asks a human for confirmation or input before proceeding, balancing automation with human oversight.
Controls placed on an agent to restrict how many API calls, tool uses, or actions it can perform in a given time period, preventing runaway costs or unintended flooding of external services.
Running agent-generated code or actions in an isolated environment so that mistakes or malicious outputs cannot affect the host system or external services.
A LangChain extension that models agent workflows as graphs, where nodes are processing steps and edges are conditional transitions — ideal for complex, looping, or multi-agent flows.
A Python framework for building multi-agent systems organized like a crew, where each agent has a defined role, goal, and set of tools, and a process coordinates their collaboration.
A Microsoft framework for multi-agent conversation, where agents exchange messages in a chat-like interface and can write and execute code to solve tasks collaboratively.
The coordinating agent in a multi-agent system responsible for planning the overall task, assigning work to specialized agents, and synthesizing their outputs into a final result.
The defined way one agent transfers a task, its context, and any intermediate results to another agent so work can continue without losing important information.
A reliability strategy where the agent automatically switches to an alternative approach — such as a different tool, model, or simpler method — when the primary one fails.
In agent systems, the ongoing observation of agent behavior, tool calls, costs, latency, and errors to detect problems, audit decisions, and improve performance over time.
A database optimized for storing and searching embeddings, used in RAG systems to find the most semantically relevant documents for a given query.
The practice of connecting LLM outputs to verified external information (via search, databases, or documents) to reduce hallucination and increase factual accuracy.
